reasoning: getUserIP() at line 145-146 reads $_SERVER['REMOTE_ADDR'] through wp_unslash() then sanitize_url(). While sanitize_url() is semantically wrong for an IP address (it is a URL sanitizer), the claimed second-order sinks do not exist in the codebase. (1) SQL: every use of $ip is passed through $wpdb->prepare() with a %s placeholder (countFails, isLockedDown) or via $wpdb->insert() which also escapes internally — no SQL injection. (2) HTML/XSS: in ajax.php lines 353-356 and 488-490, the login_attempt_IP and accesslock_IP columns are never echoed; the code substitutes hardcoded 'Available in PRO' strings, so no stored-XSS path exists in the free plugin. (3) REMOTE_ADDR is not an HTTP header and is not attacker-controlled except in misconfigured reverse-proxy setups, which is a server-level issue outside the plugin's control. (4) anonymous_logging md5() path sanitizes further. The sanitize_url() misuse is a code quality issue, but no reachable exploitable sink is present.
